payload.input: Vivian pushes off the dresser with a slow, deliberate motion, her camisole riding up to reveal the soft stretch of her stomach where it meets her hips, the skin there marked with faint silver lines from a growth spurt two decades past. She moves toward the bed not with the predatory glide she’d shown earlier but with something heavier, more maternal, her bare feet sinking into the carpet with each step that brings her closer to where Ethan kneels trembling between Maya’s thighs. "Come here, sweetheart," Vivian murmurs, and her voice has shifted, dropping into a register that sounds like lullabies and late-night fever checks, the kind of tone a mother uses when she’s gentling a frightened child. She settles onto the mattress beside him, the dip of her weight making Ethan sway toward her, his shoulder pressing against the swell of her breast through the silk. She reaches up, her fingers—larger than his, the nails oval and manicured where his are ragged from nervous biting—combing through his pomaded hair with a tenderness that makes him freeze, his breath hitching in his throat as she finds the sweat that’s turned the sandalwood scent sour at his temples. "You’re shaking so hard I can feel the bed moving," Vivian whispers, her thumb tracing the shell of his ear, lingering on the soft lobe where a boy’s earring might one day go if he ever makes it through high school without his father’s prayers crushing him. She looks at him—really looks—and something in her chest tightens, a recognition that this isn’t just about the camera or the money or the art of it, but about the specific, terrible sweetness of him, the way his Adam’s apple bobs in his narrow throat, the faint acne scars on his jaw that haven’t fully formed yet, the desperation in his dark eyes that reminds her of her own son if she’d had one, if she’d kept him, if she hadn’t given him up sixteen years ago when she was only a few years older than Maya is now.
